name: Matilda House
alias: Istana Woodneuk's quieter cousin · the Punggol mansion
body: A colonial bungalow built in the early 1900s and named for a man's late wife, it stood derelict in Punggol for decades while the district around it emptied of kampongs and then filled again with high-rise flats. In its abandoned years it was the kind of house children dared each other toward: white-walled and shuttered, set back among rank grass and old trees, close enough to the Punggol River that the old families once swam only in netted areas because of the crocodiles. The legend holds that the house was cursed, by a grieving son in some versions and a grieving mother in others, so that no one who tried to settle there would ever stay: doors slamming through the night, sleep broken without cause, the living quietly driven out. Retellings add a long-haired woman seen sitting high in a tree beside the house, glaring down at anyone who passed, and a pale mist said to drift along the corridors, an ancestral guardian who would not be moved. As a haunting it is gentler than most, a house that does not harm so much as refuse, turning away each new tenant until it is left alone again.

What makes it linger is its survival rather than its ruin. The best-known piece of lore says that when contractors came to demolish the place, workers met misfortune and bulldozers broke down or simply would not start, and the failed attempts fed the belief that the building could not be torn down at all, though there is no verified record of any death at the site to anchor the story. The documented history is quieter and stranger: the 1902 bungalow was built by the wealthy Cashin family, served as a filming location for the dramas Tenko and Tanamera, and, rather than being cleared, was gazetted for conservation and absorbed into a residential development as a restored clubhouse, a planning decision rather than a supernatural one. So while other colonial houses fell to demolition or to fire, Matilda House was kept, and the old dread now sits folded inside a modern condominium, the bungalow's white facade preserved at the development's edge like a tooth set in new gold. Residents pass it on their way to the pool and the car park; the curse, if it is a curse, has been zoned, landscaped, and given a heritage plaque, made to coexist with show-flats and morning joggers.

What remains is the strange courtesy of the arrangement, the way the city kept the haunted thing instead of clearing it, so that the story now has to be told in the present tense of property and management committees. The mansion endures as a preserved gatehouse and clubhouse for the flats behind it, lit softly at night, neither abandoned nor quite lived in, its restored shutters and trim almost too clean for the tales told about them. Tour guides still bring small groups to the railing at dusk, and the long-haired woman in the tree has not so much left as moved into the retelling, a fixture now of the estate's own folklore rather than its empty years. The children who grow up beside it learn the legend the way they learn the names of the lifts: as a fixed feature of home, a haunting with an address and a postcode, waiting in plain sight at the front of the estate.
note: Matilda House is a documented conservation case: a genuine early-twentieth-century bungalow in Punggol that was gazetted for conservation and incorporated into a later residential development rather than demolished. That much belongs to the public record of architecture and heritage policy, and is the most reliable layer of the entry.

The curse itself, the slamming doors, the tenant who could never stay, and the competing son-versus-mother origin stories belong to local lore and ghost-tour retelling, and should be presented as such rather than as fact; avoid inventing specific names, dates, or events to dramatize it. The genuinely interesting thread is how a legend adapts to redevelopment instead of dying with the building: worth tracking is how residents and tour guides now retell the story now that the house is a conserved clubhouse fronting modern flats, and how proximity and ownership reshape a haunting that was born when the place stood empty.

recountings.text: The best known piece of lore holds that when contractors tried to demolish the derelict bungalow, several workers met misfortune and machinery such as bulldozers kept breaking down or refusing to start. Locals attributed this to a vengeful or ancestral spirit guarding the house, and the failed attempts fed the belief that the building simply could not be torn down. This is folklore rather than documented record; there is no verified account of any demolition fatalities at the site.

Other retellings describe a long-haired woman seen sitting high in a tree beside the house, glaring at passers-by, along with a pale mist said to drift along the corridors. Some villagers framed her as an ancestral guardian who would harm anyone entering. The account itself notes there are no recorded murders or deaths inside the house, marking these as ghost stories rather than history.
recountings.attribution: ExplorerSG, the history behind the haunted Matilda House

recountings.text: On the documented side, National Library Singapore curator Hannah Yeo records that the 1902 bungalow was built by the wealthy Cashin family, served as a filming location for the dramas Tenko and Tanamera, and once stood close enough to the Punggol River that residents swam in netted areas because of crocodiles. Rather than being demolished, it was gazetted for conservation and absorbed into the A Treasure Trove condominium as a restored clubhouse, a planning decision rather than a supernatural one.
recountings.attribution: National Library Singapore (Hannah Yeo), Much Ado About Matilda House

references.title: Matilda House: Haunted Abandoned Millionaire's Mansion Turned Punggol Clubhouse
The History Behind the Haunted Matilda House
